coredata.dc:title: Lysosome-targeted dual-emissive carbon dots for ratiometric optical dual-readout and smartphone-assisted visual determination of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qianliang
Shi, Lihong
Wang, Xu
Zhou, Wei
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Smartphone-assisted visual assay not only expands quantitative analysis but also enhance real-time on-site sensing capabilities. Herein, lysosome-targeted dual-emissive carbon dots (L-CDs) can not only recognize Hg2+ and SO3 2− by ratiometric fluorescence and ratiometric absorption, but also visually quantify Hg2+ and SO3 2− by smartphone-assisted method. With monitoring of intrinsic ratiometric fluorescent variation (I580/I468), L-CDs are developed as an effective sensing platform for ratiometric fluorescent successive identification of Hg2+ and SO3 2− accompanying with continuous fluorescence variation of blue, purplish pink, pink, and light yellow. With detecting of inherent ratiometric absorption change (A538/A206), L-CDs are also constructed as an efficacious sensing terrace for ratiometric absorption consecutive discrimination of Hg2+ and SO3 2−. More crucially, integrating continuous fluorescence color change and color recognizer APP in the smartphone, visual quantification of Hg2+ and SO3 2− can be accomplished on the basis of the ratio of red value and blue value (R/B) with linear ranges of 0–130 and 0–850 µM, respectively, as well as LOD of 8.4 and 12.9 nM, respectively. More interestingly, confocal fluorescent imaging of HeLa cells further verifies that L-CDs can be regarded as favorable biosensors for on-site, real-time differentiation of Hg2+ and SO3 2− in vitro and in vivo with ratiometric manners.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Smartphone
Visual quantification
Hg2+
SO3 2−

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent determination of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
Ratiometric absorbable determination of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
Smartphone-assisted visual determinations of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
Visual quantification of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
Possible responding mechanism of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
Hg2+ and SO3 2– testing in tap water and lake water samples
Cell cytotoxicity and lysosome targeting of L-CDs
Determination of Hg2+ and SO3 2– In living cells
Determination of Hg2+ and SO3 2– In zebrafish larvae

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874087 ) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talent Project .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FL spectra of L-CDs with introducing different Hg2+ concentrations (0–130 μM). (B) The relationship between lg(I580/I468) and Hg2+ concentrations (0–130 μM).
(A) UV–vis spectra of L-CDs with increasing Hg2+ concentrations (0–36 μM). (B) The relationship between absorbance intensity ratios (A538/A206) and Hg2+ concentrations (0–36 μM).
(A) FL spectra of L-CDs@Hg2+ (130 μM of Hg2+) with increasing SO3 2− concentrations (0–850 μM). (B) The relationship between lg(I580/I468) and SO3 2− concentrations (0–850 μM).

(A) UV–vis spectra of L-CDs@Hg2+ (36 μM of Hg2+) with adding different concentrations of SO3 2− (0–540 μM). (B) The relationship between absorbance intensity ratios (A538/A206) and the concentrations of SO3 2− (0–540 μM).

(A) Schematic illustration of the L-CDs for visual quantification of Hg2+ and SO3 2− via the smartphone imaging. Step 1: Take photos using the camera of the smartphone; Step 2: Analyze RGB values through color recognizer application. (B) Linear relationship between R/B value and Hg2+ concentration. (Inset: Color change of L-CDs solution with various Hg2+ concentrations (0–130 μM) under 360 nm excitation). (C) Linear relationship between R/B value and SO3 2− concentration. (Inset: Color change of L-CDs@Hg2+ system with increasing SO3 2− concentrations (0–850 μM) under 360 nm excitation).

LSCM images of HeLa cells with L-CDs (A), L-CDs@Hg2+ (B), and L-CDs@Hg2++SO3 2− (C) under 405 nm laser excitation. From left to right: channel 1 (λem = 405–555 nm), channel 2 (λem = 555–700 nm), overlay of channel 1 and channel 2, and ratio.

Fluorescent imaging of zebrafish larvae with L-CDs (A), L-CDs@Hg2+ (B), and L-CDs@Hg2++SO3 2− (C) under 405 nm laser excitation. From left to right: channel 1 (λem = 405–555 nm), channel 2 (λem = 555–700 nm), bright field, overlay of channel 1 and channel 2, and ratio.
Schematic diagram of green synthesis and multifunctional application of L-CDs.

head.ce:title: Lysosome-targeted dual-emissive carbon dots for ratiometric optical dual-readout and smartphone-assisted visual determination of Hg2+ and SO3 2−

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Research Center for Information and Strategy of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Research Center for Information and Strategy of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Research Center for Information and Strategy of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Smartphone-assisted visual assay not only expands quantitative analysis but also enhance real-time on-site sensing capabilities. Herein, lysosome-targeted dual-emissive carbon dots (L-CDs) can not only recognize Hg2+ and SO3 2− by ratiometric fluorescence and ratiometric absorption, but also visually quantify Hg2+ and SO3 2− by smartphone-assisted method. With monitoring of intrinsic ratiometric fluorescent variation (I580/I468), L-CDs are developed as an effective sensing platform for ratiometric fluorescent successive identification of Hg2+ and SO3 2− accompanying with continuous fluorescence variation of blue, purplish pink, pink, and light yellow. With detecting of inherent ratiometric absorption change (A538/A206), L-CDs are also constructed as an efficacious sensing terrace for ratiometric absorption consecutive discrimination of Hg2+ and SO3 2−. More crucially, integrating continuous fluorescence color change and color recognizer APP in the smartphone, visual quantification of Hg2+ and SO3 2− can be accomplished on the basis of the ratio of red value and blue value (R/B) with linear ranges of 0–130 and 0–850 µM, respectively, as well as LOD of 8.4 and 12.9 nM, respectively. More interestingly, confocal fluorescent imaging of HeLa cells further verifies that L-CDs can be regarded as favorable biosensors for on-site, real-time differentiation of Hg2+ and SO3 2− in vitro and in vivo with ratiometric manners.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative assay has received wide attention in the fields of clinical diagnosis, environmental management, food safety, agriculture and industrial monitoring [1–3]. So far, numerous quantitative methods have been developed, including atomic absorption, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, fluorescence spectroscopy, chemiluminescence, surface-enhanced raman spectroscopy and electrochemical method [4–7]. However, the above methods demand expensive laboratory facility and outstanding specialists, which are not suitable for on-site real-time analyses. Therefore, it is the priority affair to develop a portable and quick strategy for on-site detection, notably in domains with inadequate resources.

Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging class of nanomaterials, have been favorably utilized for a variety of practical applications including fluorescent sensing, biological labeling, gene delivery, dye sensitizers, fluorescent ink, and drug delivery by virtue of fabulous photoluminescence, high photo-stability, excellent water solubility, low toxicity, and advantageous biocompatibility [8–13]. In recent years, green synthesis of CDs has attracted widespread attention due to people’s concern about environmental pollution. Liao et al. reported a simple and green strategy to prepare N-doped CDs from the mixture of natural peach gum polysaccharide and ethylenediamine to detect Au3+ [14]. Hu et al. developed blue-emissive CDs using flax straw by hydrothermal method with an outstanding sensing ability for Co2+, Cr6+, and ascorbic acid (AA) [15]. Jagannathan et al. provided a facile synthesis of white light-emitting CDs from hydrothermal treatment of corncob for DNA, paracetamol, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ sensing [16]. However, most of CDs from green sources are generally designed as intensity-based probes, which are often affected by some unavoidable factors such as background signal, photobleaching, fluctuation of light source, and sensibility of instruments [17–19]. Ratiometric fluorescent sensors can resolve above problem by measuring the ratio of fluorescence (FL) intensities of two well-resolved emission peaks [20]. Currently, green syntheses of CDs have been exploited for ratiometric fluorescent sensing. Wang et al. synthesized dual-emitting CDs through solvothermal method from holly leaves for ratiometric fluorescent monitoring Hg2+ in living cells [21]. Zhu et al. proposed green preparation of CDs from plum as a ratiometric fluorescent probe to detect doxorubicin [22]. However, the study on green synthetic CDs for ratiometric fluorescent sensing is still relatively little, and thus there is much research space.

Mercury ion (Hg2+), one of heavy metal ions with high toxicity, can enter human body through skin, respiratory system or food and cause the disorder and damage of DNA, central nervous systems and endocrine systems [23–27]. According to WHO recommendations, a maximum acceptable Hg2+ concentration has been set to be 1 μg L−1 in drinking water [28]. In addition, due to the durability and non-biodegradability of Hg2+, waste water and soil contaminated with Hg2+ are difficult to purify [29]. So far, several analytical methods of Hg2+ have been reported, including atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductive coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry, X-ray absorption spectroscopy, and surface enhanced Raman scattering [30–33]. However, these methods require complex preparation scheme, expensive laboratory instrument, and time-consuming analysis [34–36]. Consequently, researchers are trying to develop or design chemical sensors for sensitively analyzing Hg2+ in a direct and effective way. Recently, CDs-based nanosensors concerning Hg2+ discrimination have been exploited. Li et al. synthesized blue fluorescent N-doped CDs using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and fluorescein by one-step hydrothermal strategy for detecting Hg2+ [37]. Wang et al. fabricated blue fluorescent CDs from DL-thioctic acid to distinguish Hg2+ [38]. Xie et al. fabricated dual-emission CDs-gold nanoclusters nanohybrid, which was used in visual and ratiometric fluorescent detection of Hg2+ [39]. Nevertheless, these Hg2+ fluorescent sensors are either single CDs-based intensity-modulated or ratiometric fluorescent by self-assembling or covalently conjugating CDs with another fluorescent material. Thus, it is of great significance to develop single CDs based Hg2+ ratiometric fluorescent sensors.

Sulfite (SO3 2−), one of sulfur dioxide derivatives, plays a vital role in disease diagnosis [40]. At present, SO3 2− levels have been strictly controlled. According to the Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives recommendations, our acceptable daily intake of SO3 2− is 0.7 mg kg−1 of body weight [41]. Abnormally high level of SO3 2− is closely related to respiratory, neurological, and cardiovascular diseases [42–44]. Therefore, it is of essential and urgent require to seek sensitive and effective SO3 2− detection methods. Recently, fluorescent probes have been recognized as effective tools for identifying SO3 2−. Xu et al. employed a new carbazole-benzo[e]indolium dye as a ratiometric fluorescent and chromogenic probe for SO3 2− detection [40]. Zhang et al. developed a new water soluble and fluorogenic probe using 3-ethyl-1,1,2-trimethyl-1H-benzo[e]indol-3-ium and isophthalaldehyde as raw materials for tracing SO3 2− [45]. Samanta et al. designed 4-methylbenzo[4,5]imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-3-carbaldehyde as a colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probe to discern SO3 2− [46]. However, CDs-based SO3 2− ratiometric fluorescent sensors have scarcely been reported. Therefore, it is extremely imperative to excavate CDs-based SO3 2− ratiometric fluorescent sensors.

Herein, lysosome-targeted dual-emissive carbon dots (L-CDs) have been fabricated by hydrothermal treatment using natural hematoxylin as only precursor. Synthesized L-CDs can be constructed as a triple-signal platform for ratiometric FL, ratiometric absorption, and smartphone-assisted visual determinations of Hg2+ and SO3 2– in watery solution. Attractively, the applicability of L-CDs, as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for sequential differentiation Hg2+ and SO3 2– in actual water samples, is demonstrated with satisfactory recoveries in spiked water samples, which broadens their applications for environmental sensing in the future. Furthermore, as-prepared L-CDs can be regarded as available biosensors to construct dual-sensing terraces for sequential tracking of Hg2+ and SO3 2– in HeLa cells and zebrafish larvae (Scheme 1 ). Compared with previous reports on FL sensors, innovation points of this work lie in following aspects: (1) Ratiometric FL L-CDs were ingeniously developed by one pot hydrothermal method. (2) The L-CDs accomplished visual quantification of Hg2+ and SO3 2– by integrating with smartphone. (3) Visual sensing of Hg2+ and SO3 2– was achieved in HeLa cells and zebrafish larvae.

L-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of natural hematoxylin. Briefly, 0.4 g of natural hematoxylin and 30 mL of ultrapure water were mixed, and then the cloudy solution was placed in an oven for 3 h at 220 °C. After allowing the reactants to cool naturally, L-CDs solution was collected after centrifuging, filtering, and dialyzing. Obtained L-CDs was preserved in reserve at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent determination of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Hg2+, 200 μL of L-CDs solution was added to 2 mL of PBS solution at pH 7, and then different concentrations of Hg2+ standards were introduced. For the monitoring of SO3 2−, different concentrations of SO3 2− standards were sequentially added into L-CDs@Hg2+ system (0.008 g/L of L-CDs solution, 130 μM of Hg2+ standards). FL spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric absorbable determination of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Hg2+, 27 μL of L-CDs solution was added to 2 mL of PBS solution at pH 7, and then different concentrations of Hg2+ standards were introduced. For the detection of SO3 2−, different concentrations of SO3 2− standards were added to L-CDs@Hg2+ solution one by one (0.001 g/L of L-CDs solution, 36 μM of Hg2+ standards). UV–vis spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted visual determinations of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
ce:section.ce:para: 200 μL of L-CDs solution was added to 2 mL of PBS solution at pH 7, then, Hg2+ standard solution with different concentrations were introduced. FL color changes of solution were recorded under 360 nm excitation. Then, FL colors were recognized through a color recognizer APP in the smartphone and converted into RGB values. According to the linear relationship between the ratios of R value and B value (R/B) and Hg2+ concentrations, the visual quantification of Hg2+ can be quantified.

For sensing of SO3 2−, different concentrations of SO3 2− standard solution were introduced to L-CDs@Hg2+ (130 μM of Hg2+) solution. FL color changes of solution were recorded upon 360 nm excitation. The visual quantification of SO3 2− could be achieved through the linear relationship between R/B values and SO3 2− concentrations.

As demonstrated in Fig. S1A–C, TEM images of L-CDs of distinct scales exhibit seude-spherical appearance and fantastic dispersion of L-CDs. Fig. S1D illustrates that L-CDs’ average diameter is about 2.15 nm. AFM image of L-CDs (Fig. S2) depicts a spherical-like shape with even height of ca. 3.10 nm. Coincident results are obtained with TEM.

L-CDs’ surface structure was surveyed in depth through XPS and FTIR (Fig. S3). Fig. S3A presents XPS full spectra of L-CDs with two chief bands of C1s (281.8 eV) and O1s (530.3 eV). C1s spectrum of L-CDs (Fig. S3B) is separated into four peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.4 eV, 288.0 eV and 289.2 eV, which could be ascribed to CC, CN, CO and OCO, respectively. O1s spectrum of L-CDs (Fig. S3C) is divided into two bands at 532.1 eV and 533.0 eV, attributable to CO and COH/COC. N1s spectrum of L-CDs (Fig. S3D) is fitted into two peaks at 399.3 eV and 400.7 eV, ascribing to CN and N(C3), respectively. FTIR spectrum is shown in Fig. S3E. A peak at 3399.3 cm−1 shows the OH stretching vibration. The peak at 2414.0 cm−1 ascribes to CH stretching vibration. An absorption band at 1790.5 cm−1 confirms the existence of COO−. The absorption band at 1362.8 cm−1 corresponds to CN. A peak at 1152.5 cm−1 is assigned to COC stretching vibration. A peak at 1066.3 cm−1 is associated with CO stretching vibration. The absorption band at 941.4 cm−1 is attributed to CO stretching vibration. An absorption peak at 858.3 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CH. Above results of FTIR and XPS act in cooperation with each other.

Hematoxylin, as a green carbon source, was utilized to fabricate L-CDs by one-pot hydrothermal method, which endows obtained L-CDs with bright blue emission and weak yellow emission (Fig. S4A and S4B). UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S4A) indicates plainly two characteristic absorption peaks at 216 nm and 294 nm, corresponding with π-π* transition of CC of localized sp2 clusters in the core and n-π* transition of CO of carboxyl groups on L-CDs’ surface [47]. L-CDs’ average FL lifetime is calculated to be 2.9537 ns (Fig. S4C). FL quantum yield of L-CDs is estimated as 17.8 % with quinine sulfate as a reference. As shown in Fig. S5, L-CDs’ FL intensity illustrates no significant fluctuations under different NaCl concentrations (0–10 mM), which certificates potential application of L-CDs in physiological salt environments. What’s more, L-CDs’ FL intensity remains steady with pH values from 3 to 10 and after storage times for 9 days (Fig. S5).

To investigate L-CDs’ selectivity, the effects of cations, anions and amino acids on FL intensity were examined under pH 7 at 360 nm excitation. Lg(I580/I468) of L-CDs increases significantly after the addition of Hg2+, while the similar phenomenon does not occur after introducing other competitive substances (Fig. S6), testifying admirable selectivity of L-CDs for Hg2+. Additionally, the presence of interfering substances (130 μM) does not show obvious interference with the monitoring of Hg2+.

Ratiometric fluorescent sensing capability of L-CDs for Hg2+ ([Hg2+] = 0–130 μM) was implemented upon pH 7 at 360 nm excitation (Fig. 1 ). When Hg2+ concentrations increase, blue peak of L-CDs at 468 nm decreases gradually while a ratio rising yellow emission band at 580 nm enhances little by little (Fig. 1A). An excellent linear relationship with R2 = 0.9950 between lg(I580/I468) and Hg2+ concentrations is displayed in the range of 5–130 μM (Fig. 1B), and the extremely low limits of detection (LOD) of 2.5 nM is obtained on basis of the formula LOD = 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation for the FL intensity of the blank solution, and k is the slope of the fitted curve. Compared with reported CDs-based Hg2+ sensors, the proposed Hg2+ fluorescent probe exhibits superior sensitivity (Table S1).

Ratiometric absorption sensing capability of L-CDs for Hg2+ ([Hg2+] = 0–36 μM) was investigated upon pH 7 (Fig. 2 ). With increasing Hg2+ amounts, L-CDs’ absorbance intensity at 206 nm reduces progressively while that at 538 nm increases gradually (Fig. 2A). A super linear correlationship is shown between absorbance intensity ratios (A538/A206) and Hg2+ concentrations in the range of 3–36 μM with R2 = 0.9962 in Fig. 2B. Besides, the LOD is calculated to be 0.5 μM.

To assess the selectivity of L-CDs@Hg2+, the effects of positive ions, negative ions and amino acids on FL intensity were implemented (Fig. S7). Lg(I580/I468) of L-CDs@Hg2+ reduces distinctly upon the introduction of SO3 2−, but analogous phenomenon fails to appear upon the addition of other competitive substances, implying outstanding selectivity of L-CDs@Hg2+ for SO3 2−. Furthermore, other high-concentration (up to 850 μM) competitive substances do not significantly interfere with SO3 2− detection.

Ratiometric fluorescent sensing capability of L-CDs@Hg2+ (130 μM of Hg2+) for SO3 2− ([SO3 2−] = 50–850 μM) was surveyed upon pH 7 under 360 nm excitation (Fig. 3 ). With increasing SO3 2− concentrations, FL intensity of L-CDs@Hg2+ at 468 nm enhances gradually while that at 580 nm weakens progressively (Fig. 3A). A good linear relationship between lg(I580/I468) and SO3 2− concentrations is fitted in the range of 50–850 μM (R2 = 0.9904, Fig. 3B). The LOD is computed to be 27.6 nM, which is evidently lower than that of published articles for detecting SO3 2− (Table S2).

Ratiometric absorption sensing capability of L-CDs@Hg2+ (36 μM of Hg2+) system for SO3 2− ([SO3 2−] = 0–540 μM) was implemented upon pH 7 (Fig. 4 ). With ascending SO3 2− concentrations, the absorbance intensity at 538 nm decreases progressively but a ratio increases at 206 nm (Fig. 4A). Two fine linear relationships between absorbance intensity ratios (A538/A206) and SO3 2− concentrations (Fig. 4B) are obtained in the ranges of 30–240 μM and 360–540 μM with R2 of 0.9848 and 0.9880, respectively. Besides, the LOD is calculated as 3.5 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual quantification of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the importance of simplifying the detection process and operation in practical applications, a smartphone-based sensing platform was cultivated for achieving rapid detections of Hg2+ and SO3 2−. Fig. 5 A authenticates the process of detecting Hg2+ and SO3 2− based on the smartphone imaging. The color of L-CDs solution changes incrementally from blue, purplish pink, pink to light yellow under 360 nm excitation with increasing Hg2+ concentration from 0 to 130 μM (Inset of Fig. 5B). FL colors could be transformed into RGB values through a color recognizer application in the smartphone, of which the ratio of R/B could be employed as a parameter for Hg2+ quantification. Fig. 5B shows a satisfactory linear relationship between R/B values and Hg2+ concentrations from 0 to 130 μM, as well as LOD of 8.4 nM. Moreover, the color of L-CDs@Hg2+ solution returns gradually from light yellow, pink purplish, pink to blue under 360 nm excitation when SO3 2− concentration changes from 0 to 850 μM (Inset of Fig. 5C). And R/B values make known a terrific linear relationship with SO3 2− concentrations varying from 0 to 850 μM (Fig. 5C), as well as LOD of 12.9 nM. The above results show that L-CDs can be developed as optical mode and smartphone imaging platforms for ratiometric detection and visual quantification of Hg2+ and SO3 2−.

To investigate the effect of pH, the fluorescent assay of Hg2+ and SO3 2− was carried out at different pH conditions. The results indicate that the optimal pH of detecting Hg2+ and SO3 2− is 7 (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible responding mechanism of Hg2+ and SO3 2−
ce:section.ce:para: For Hg2+, 3d orbits are fully filled. Its empty 4 s and 4p orbits can hybridize to form equivalent sp3 hybrid orbits, which can accept the lone pair electrons of the ligands to form a tetra-coordinated compound. L-CDs’ surface is covered with carboxyl groups, in which O contains lone pair electrons. Thus, we deduce that the sensor response to Hg2+ can be attributed to the coordination between L-CDs and Hg2+ because the lone pair electrons of O enter into the sp3 hybrid orbits of Hg2+. To corroborate Hg2+ sensing mechanism, zeta potential, FTIR, TEM, and AFM were investigated. Zeta potential (Fig. S8) of L-CDs increases dramatically from −0.23 to +0.11 mV upon adding Hg2+, authenticating the successful coordination of Hg2+ with L-CDs. In contrast with L-CDs’ FTIR spectrum, the appearance of a new absorption band at 1664.7 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum of L-CDs@Hg2+ verifies the formation of HgO bond (Fig. S9). Moreover, TEM (Fig. S10) and AFM (Fig. S11) exhibit the change of L-CDs’ size from 3.18 to 7.51 nm and from 3.10 to 8.23 nm upon introducing Hg2+, respectively, indicating that the coordination of Hg2+ with L-CDs induces L-CDs’ aggregation. According to above analyses, on one hand, Hg2+ gives rise to charge/electron transfer between Hg2+ and L-CDs, weakening blue emission of L-CDs. On the other hand, Hg2+ induced aggregation of L-CDs restricts the rotation of the functional groups on L-CDs’ surface, decreases the non-radiative transition rate, and increases the radiative transition rate, enhancing yellow emission of L-CDs.

For L-CDs@Hg2+, the introduction of SO3 2− can render redox reaction between Hg2+ and SO3 2− occur, in which Hg2+ is reduced to Hg2 2+ and SO3 2− is oxidized to SO4 2−. To verify the speculation, zeta potential, FTIR, TEM, and AFM were executed. SO3 2− makes zeta potential of L-CDs@Hg2+ vary from +0.11 to −0.19 mV (Fig. S8), which confirms the separation of Hg2+ from L-CDs’ surface due to the occurrence of reduction reaction of Hg2+. Contrasted with FTIR spectrum of L-CDs@Hg2+, introducing SO3 2− renders the new peak at 1664.7 cm−1 disappear again (Fig. S9), corroborating the fracture of HgO bond. As illustrated in TEM and AFM (Figs. S10 and S11), the addition of SO3 2− makes the particle size of L-CDs@Hg2+ decrease from 7.51 to 2.17 nm and from 8.23 to 4.21 nm, respectively, which reveals that the aggregation of L-CDs releases. Therefore, ensuing addition of SO3 2− to L-CDs@Hg2+ solution not only forbids charge/electron transfer between Hg2+ and L-CDs, enhancing blue FL, but also increases the non-radiative transition rate and decreases the radiative transition rate, fading yellow FL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+ and SO3 2– testing in tap water and lake water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent detection method, standard recovery experiments were operated in pretreated tap water and lake water. When Hg2+ concentrations are 10, 50 and 100 μM in pretreated tap water, the recoveries are found to be 98.82 %, 101.22 %, and 104.93 %, respectively (Table S3). Analogously, as 100, 400 and 800 μM of SO3 2– are added, the recoveries are found to be 95.59 %, 98.87 %, and 104.20 % (Table S3). Besides, when Hg2+ concentrations are 10, 50 and 100 μM in pretreated lake water, the recoveries are found to be 98.30 %, 99.02 %, and 102.79 %, respectively (Table S3). Analogously, as 100, 400 and 800 μM of SO3 2– are added, the recoveries are found to be 98.13 %, 101.37 %, and 99.47 % (Table S4). The recoveries of Hg2+ and SO3 2– verify that the proposed ratiometric fluorescent detection method may be utilized for practical detections of Hg2+ and SO3 2– in real samples, showing great application prospects in the field of environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity and lysosome targeting of L-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: L-CDs’ cytotoxicity was evaluated in HeLa cells via MTT assay to develop the application of L-CDs in biological imaging. More than 86.7 % cell survival (even at 20 mg/L of L-CDs) confirms L-CDs possess low cytotoxicity and suitable applications for cell imaging (Fig. S12). In addition, lysosome-targeting ability of L-CDs were conducted by co-localization experiments using Lyso Tracker Green. L-CDs in HeLa cells have considerable overlap with Lyso Tracker Green probe (Fig. S13). Pearson correlation coefficient is 0.9, which reveals that L-CDs have super lysosome targeting ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and SO3 2– In living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) imaging of HeLa cells (Fig. 6 ) was achieved under 405 nm laser excitation by collecting blue channel (λem = 405–555 nm) and yellow channel (λem = 555–700 nm). L-CDs-stained HeLa cells present bright blue FL but weak yellow one (Fig. 6A). Upon adding Hg2+, yellow FL increases while blue one fades (Fig. 6B). With subsequent introducing of SO3 2–, blue FL enhances but yellow one weakens (Fig. 6C). Furthermore, the intracellular FL intensities of L-CDs, L-CDs@Hg2+ and L-CDs@Hg2++SO3 2− are displayed in Fig. S14. And the inset of Fig. S14 presents the lg(I580/I468) of L-CDs, L-CDs@Hg2+ and L-CDs@Hg2++SO3 2−, respectively. Above results prove that L-CDs could be constructed as a visualization ratiometric fluorescent biosensing platform for consecutive discerning Hg2+ and SO3 2− in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and SO3 2– In zebrafish larvae
ce:section.ce:para: More attractively, we studied the usefulness of L-CDs in zebrafish larvae to widen their applications. Viability assay of zebrafish larvae was initially performed at different concentration of L-CDs for 24 h. The results exhibit that more than 82.7 % of zebrafish larvae are viable even at 20 mg/L L-CDs (Fig. S16), implying low toxicity of L-CDs to zebrafish larvae. Fig. 7 A indicates zebrafish larvae labeled with L-CDs represents bright blue emission and weak yellow emission. Fascinatingly, upon the introduction of Hg2+, a significant reduction of blue emission and an obvious ascension of yellow emission are presented (Fig. 7B). Subsequent addition of SO3 2– makes blue FL enhance and yellow FL fade (Fig. 7C). The above results indicate that L-CDs can successfully serve as a visual ratiometric fluorescent biosensor for monitoring the change of Hg2+ and SO3 2– in vivo.

L-CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal threatment using natural hematoxylin as single precursor. As-prepared L-CDs exhibit admirable photoluminescence, outstanding water solubility, low toxicity and superior biocompatibility. Benefitting from these favorable traits, L-CDs has been successfully designed as three-signal sensing platforms for ratiometric FL, ratiometric absorption and smartphone-assisted visual determinations of Hg2+ and SO3 2− in aqueous solution. At the same time, L-CDs have successfully accomplished the analyses of Hg2+ and SO3 2− in real water samples. More importantly, as-obtained L-CDs could be applied as a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for in vitro and in vivo tracking of Hg2+ and SO3 2−, which demonstrates that L-CDs have tremendous potential in clinical diagnosis and biomedical fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talent Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123573.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
